title: Tesla driver faces manslaughter charges over Texas crash that killed a woman inside her home
content: The man whose Tesla struck and killed a woman inside her Texas home last month is now facing manslaughter charges, as reported earlier by The Wall Street Journal and local news outlet KHOU 11. 44-year-old Michael Butler was arrested on Wednesday and claimed to have been driving his Model 3 using Tesla's Full-Self Driving (FSD) […]
summary: ['Michael Butler was arrested and charged with manslaughter after his Tesla Model 3 struck and killed a woman inside her Texas home.', "The incident involved the use of Tesla's Full-Self Driving (FSD) system, which Butler claimed to have been using at the time.", "The case highlights ongoing concerns and legal scrutiny surrounding Tesla's FSD technology and its safety implications."]
insight: The incident underscores potential risks and accountability issues related to the deployment of Tesla's Full-Self Driving system in real-world scenarios, especially when accidents occur involving autonomous or semi-autonomous driving features.
impact: This case may influence regulatory and legal approaches to autonomous vehicle technologies, potentially affecting Tesla's FSD deployment and public trust in self-driving systems.

title: I like fish, especially dolphins: Addressing Contradictions in Dialogue Modeling
content: To quantify how well natural language understanding models can capture consistency in a general conversation, we introduce the DialoguE COntradiction DEtection task (DECODE) and a new conversational dataset containing both human-human and human-bot contradictory dialogues. We then compare a structured utterance-based approach of using pre-trained Transformer models for contradiction detection with the typical unstructured approach. Results reveal that: (i) our newly collected dataset is notably more effective at providing supervision for the dialogue contradiction detection task than existing NLI data including those aimed to cover the dialogue domain; (ii) the structured utterance-based approach is more robust and transferable on both analysis and out-of-distribution dialogues than its unstructured counterpart. We also show that our best contradiction detection model correlates well with human judgments and further provide evidence for its usage in both automatically evaluating and improving the consistency of state-of-the-art generative chatbots.
summary: ['Introduced the DialoguE COntradiction DEtection task (DECODE) to evaluate natural language understanding models on detecting contradictions in conversations.', 'Created a new conversational dataset containing both human-human and human-bot contradictory dialogues.', 'Compared a structured utterance-based approach using pre-trained Transformer models against typical unstructured methods for contradiction detection.', 'Found that the new dataset provides more effective supervision for dialogue contradiction detection than existing NLI datasets, even those targeting dialogue domains.']
insight: A structured utterance-based approach leveraging pre-trained Transformer models, combined with a newly curated dataset of contradictory dialogues, significantly improves the detection of contradictions in conversational contexts compared to unstructured methods and existing NLI datasets.
impact: This work advances the ability of AI systems to understand and maintain consistency in conversations, which is crucial for improving dialogue agents' reliability and user trust in natural language interactions.

title: Claude Fable 5 will be available again globally tomorrow. After a series of productive conversations with the US government, we're redeploying the model with a new set of classifiers to target and block more cybersecurity tasks. In the near term, some routine tasks like coding and debugging will fall back to Opus 4.8. We’ll continue to refine these classifiers over the coming weeks to reduce false positives and better distinguish genuine misuse from legitimate requests. We’ve also begun drafting a consensus framework—with Amazon, Microsoft, Google, and other Glasswing partners—for assessing the severity of AI jailbreaks and how AI developers should respond to them. We invite other industry partners and model providers to join us in this effort. Finally, we’re scaling up our collaboration with the US government on model testing and safeguards. This will include pre-release access to models and safeguards for evaluation, information sharing on jailbreaks and misuse, and dedicated resources for joint research. Thank you to our users for your patience, and to our partners across the government, industry, and the research community who worked alongside us to make Fable 5 available again. Read our full blog: https://www.anthropic.com/news/redeploying-fable-5
content:
Claude Fable 5 will be available again globally tomorrow.

After a series of productive conversations with the US government, we're redeploying the model with a new set of classifiers to target and block more cybersecurity tasks. In the near term, some routine tasks like coding and debugging will fall back to Opus 4.8. We’ll continue to refine these classifiers over the coming weeks to reduce false positives and better distinguish genuine misuse from legitimate requests.

We’ve also begun drafting a consensus framework—with Amazon, Microsoft, Google, and other Glasswing partners—for assessing the severity of AI jailbreaks and how AI developers should respond to them. We invite other industry partners and model providers to join us in this effort.

Finally, we’re scaling up our collaboration with the US government on model testing and safeguards. This will include pre-release access to models and safeguards for evaluation, information sharing on jailbreaks and misuse, and dedicated resources for joint research.

Thank you to our users for your patience, and to our partners across the government, industry, and the research community who worked alongside us to make Fable 5 available again.

Read our full blog: anthropic.com/news/redeployi…

title: I like fish, especially dolphins: Addressing Contradictions in Dialogue Modeling
content: To quantify how well natural language understanding models can capture consistency in a general conversation, we introduce the DialoguE COntradiction DEtection task (DECODE) and a new conversational dataset containing both human-human and human-bot contradictory dialogues. We then compare a structured utterance-based approach of using pre-trained Transformer models for contradiction detection with the typical unstructured approach. Results reveal that: (i) our newly collected dataset is notably more effective at providing supervision for the dialogue contradiction detection task than existing NLI data including those aimed to cover the dialogue domain; (ii) the structured utterance-based approach is more robust and transferable on both analysis and out-of-distribution dialogues than its unstructured counterpart. We also show that our best contradiction detection model correlates well with human judgments and further provide evidence for its usage in both automatically evaluating and improving the consistency of state-of-the-art generative chatbots.
summary: ['Introduced the DialoguE COntradiction DEtection task (DECODE) along with a new conversational dataset featuring human-human and human-bot contradictory dialogues.', 'Compared structured utterance-based Transformer models against unstructured approaches for detecting contradictions in dialogue.', 'Found that the new dataset provides better supervision for dialogue contradiction detection than existing NLI datasets.', 'Demonstrated that the structured utterance-based approach is more robust and transferable across different dialogue types.', 'Showed that the best contradiction detection model aligns well with human judgments and can be used to evaluate and improve chatbot consistency.']
insight: A structured utterance-based approach using pre-trained Transformer models, trained on a newly collected contradiction dialogue dataset, significantly improves robustness and transferability in detecting contradictions compared to unstructured methods and existing NLI datasets.
impact: This work advances the ability to automatically detect inconsistencies in conversational AI, enabling better evaluation and enhancement of chatbot dialogue consistency, which is critical for more reliable and coherent human-AI interactions.

title: Compliance Generation for Privacy Documents under GDPR: A Roadmap for Implementing Automation and Machine Learning
content: Most prominent research today addresses compliance with data protection laws through consumer-centric and public-regulatory approaches. We shift this perspective with the Privatech project to focus on corporations and law firms as agents of compliance. To comply with data protection laws, data processors must implement accountability measures to assess and document compliance in relation to both privacy documents and privacy practices. In this paper, we survey, on the one hand, current research on GDPR automation, and on the other hand, the operational challenges corporations face to comply with GDPR, and that may benefit from new forms of automation. We attempt to bridge the gap. We provide a roadmap for compliance assessment and generation by identifying compliance issues, breaking them down into tasks that can be addressed through machine learning and automation, and providing notes about related developments in the Privatech project.
summary: ['The Privatech project shifts GDPR compliance focus from consumers to corporations and law firms as compliance agents.', 'Data processors must implement accountability measures to assess and document compliance with privacy laws.', 'The paper surveys current research on GDPR automation and operational challenges faced by corporations.', 'It proposes a roadmap to break down compliance issues into tasks addressable by machine learning and automation.', 'The work aims to bridge the gap between research and practical corporate compliance needs.']
insight: By decomposing GDPR compliance into discrete tasks suitable for machine learning and automation, the Privatech project enables systematic assessment and generation of compliance documentation tailored for corporate and legal actors.
impact: This approach can improve the efficiency and accuracy of corporate GDPR compliance, reducing legal risks and operational burdens while advancing automation in privacy law adherence.

title: Toward Compact Data from Big Data
content: Bigdata is a dataset of which size is beyond the ability of handling a valuable raw material that can be refined and distilled into valuable specific insights. Compact data is a method that optimizes the big dataset that gives best assets without handling complex bigdata. The compact dataset contains the maximum knowledge patterns at fine grained level for effective and personalized utilization of bigdata systems without bigdata. The compact data method is a tailor-made design which depends on problem situations. Various compact data techniques have been demonstrated into various data-driven research area in the paper.
summary: ['Big data refers to datasets too large to handle directly but contains valuable raw information.', 'Compact data is a method to optimize big datasets by extracting maximum knowledge patterns at a fine-grained level.', 'This approach enables effective and personalized utilization of big data systems without managing the full big data.', 'Compact data methods are tailor-made depending on specific problem situations.', 'The paper demonstrates various compact data techniques applied across different data-driven research areas.']
insight: Compact data techniques distill large datasets into smaller, knowledge-rich subsets that retain essential patterns, enabling efficient and personalized data analysis without the overhead of handling full-scale big data.
impact: This approach reduces computational complexity and resource requirements for big data analysis, making it more accessible and practical for diverse applications and personalized solutions.

title: Neural document expansion for ad-hoc information retrieval
content: Recently, Nogueira et al. [2019] proposed a new approach to document expansion based on a neural Seq2Seq model, showing significant improvement on short text retrieval task. However, this approach needs a large amount of in-domain training data. In this paper, we show that this neural document expansion approach can be effectively adapted to standard IR tasks, where labels are scarce and many long documents are present.
summary: ['Nogueira et al. (2019) introduced a neural Seq2Seq model for document expansion that improves short text retrieval.', 'The original approach requires a large amount of in-domain training data.', 'This paper demonstrates that the neural document expansion method can be adapted effectively to standard information retrieval (IR) tasks.', 'The adaptation works well even when labeled data is scarce and documents are long.']
insight: Neural Seq2Seq-based document expansion can be adapted to standard IR tasks with limited labeled data and long documents, overcoming the original method's dependency on large in-domain datasets.
impact: This adaptation broadens the applicability of neural document expansion techniques to more realistic IR scenarios where labeled data is limited and documents are lengthy, potentially improving retrieval performance in practical settings.

title: Feature Selection and Feature Extraction in Pattern Analysis: A Literature Review
content: Pattern analysis often requires a pre-processing stage for extracting or selecting features in order to help the classification, prediction, or clustering stage discriminate or represent the data in a better way. The reason for this requirement is that the raw data are complex and difficult to process without extracting or selecting appropriate features beforehand. This paper reviews theory and motivation of different common methods of feature selection and extraction and introduces some of their applications. Some numerical implementations are also shown for these methods. Finally, the methods in feature selection and extraction are compared.
summary: ['The article reviews the theory and motivation behind common feature selection and extraction methods used in pattern analysis.', 'It emphasizes the importance of pre-processing raw data to improve classification, prediction, and clustering tasks.', 'Numerical implementations of various feature selection and extraction techniques are demonstrated.', 'A comparative analysis of these methods is provided to highlight their strengths and applications.']
insight: Effective feature selection and extraction significantly enhance the ability of pattern analysis algorithms to discriminate and represent complex raw data, improving downstream tasks such as classification and clustering.
impact: By systematically reviewing and comparing feature selection and extraction methods, the paper aids researchers and practitioners in choosing appropriate pre-processing techniques, leading to better model performance and more efficient data analysis.

title: Conditioning LSTM Decoder and Bi-directional Attention Based Question Answering System
content: Applying neural-networks on Question Answering has gained increasing popularity in recent years. In this paper, I implemented a model with Bi-directional attention flow layer, connected with a Multi-layer LSTM encoder, connected with one start-index decoder and one conditioning end-index decoder. I introduce a new end-index decoder layer, conditioning on start-index output. The Experiment shows this has increased model performance by 15.16%. For prediction, I proposed a new smart-span equation, rewarding both short answer length and high probability in start-index and end-index, which further improved the prediction accuracy. The best single model achieves an F1 score of 73.97% and EM score of 64.95% on test set.
summary: ['Implemented a question answering model using a Bi-directional attention flow layer combined with a multi-layer LSTM encoder.', 'Introduced a novel end-index decoder layer conditioned on the start-index output, improving model performance by 15.16%.', 'Proposed a new smart-span prediction equation that balances short answer length and high start/end index probabilities, enhancing accuracy.', 'Achieved a best single model F1 score of 73.97% and Exact Match (EM) score of 64.95% on the test set.']
insight: Conditioning the end-index decoder on the start-index output allows the model to better capture dependencies between answer boundaries, significantly boosting performance. Additionally, the smart-span prediction method optimizes answer span selection by jointly considering answer length and probability scores.
impact: This approach advances question answering model architectures by improving answer boundary prediction and span selection, leading to higher accuracy. It demonstrates a practical method to enhance neural QA systems, which can be applied to improve real-world QA applications.

title: Robust Federated Training via Collaborative Machine Teaching using Trusted Instances
content: Federated learning performs distributed model training using local data hosted by agents. It shares only model parameter updates for iterative aggregation at the server. Although it is privacy-preserving by design, federated learning is vulnerable to noise corruption of local agents, as demonstrated in the previous study on adversarial data poisoning threat against federated learning systems. Even a single noise-corrupted agent can bias the model training. In our work, we propose a collaborative and privacy-preserving machine teaching paradigm with multiple distributed teachers, to improve robustness of the federated training process against local data corruption. We assume that each local agent (teacher) have the resources to verify a small portions of trusted instances, which may not by itself be adequate for learning. In the proposed collaborative machine teaching method, these trusted instances guide the distributed agents to jointly select a compact while informative training subset from data hosted by their own. Simultaneously, the agents learn to add changes of limited magnitudes into the selected data instances, in order to improve the testing performances of the federally trained model despite of the training data corruption. Experiments on toy and real data demonstrate that our approach can identify training set bugs effectively and suggest appropriate changes to the labels. Our algorithm is a step toward trustworthy machine learning.
summary: ['Federated learning enables distributed model training using local data while preserving privacy by sharing only model updates.', 'Federated learning is vulnerable to noise corruption from local agents, which can bias the global model.', 'The authors propose a collaborative, privacy-preserving machine teaching paradigm with multiple distributed teachers to improve robustness against local data corruption.', 'Each local agent verifies a small portion of trusted instances to guide selection of a compact, informative training subset.', 'The method allows agents to make limited changes to selected data instances to improve model performance despite corrupted training data.']
insight: By leveraging small sets of trusted data across distributed agents and collaboratively selecting and adjusting training subsets, the proposed machine teaching approach enhances federated learning robustness against adversarial data corruption without compromising privacy.
impact: This approach addresses a critical vulnerability in federated learning systems, improving their reliability and trustworthiness in real-world applications where local data may be corrupted or adversarially manipulated.

title: Image Matters: Scalable Detection of Offensive and Non-Compliant Content / Logo in Product Images
content: In e-commerce, product content, especially product images have a significant influence on a customer's journey from product discovery to evaluation and finally, purchase decision. Since many e-commerce retailers sell items from other third-party marketplace sellers besides their own, the content published by both internal and external content creators needs to be monitored and enriched, wherever possible. Despite guidelines and warnings, product listings that contain offensive and non-compliant images continue to enter catalogs. Offensive and non-compliant content can include a wide range of objects, logos, and banners conveying violent, sexually explicit, racist, or promotional messages. Such images can severely damage the customer experience, lead to legal issues, and erode the company brand. In this paper, we present a computer vision driven offensive and non-compliant image detection system for extremely large image datasets. This paper delves into the unique challenges of applying deep learning to real-world product image data from retail world. We demonstrate how we resolve a number of technical challenges such as lack of training data, severe class imbalance, fine-grained class definitions etc. using a number of practical yet unique technical strategies. Our system combines state-of-the-art image classification and object detection techniques with budgeted crowdsourcing to develop a solution customized for a massive, diverse, and constantly evolving product catalog.
summary: ['The paper addresses the problem of detecting offensive and non-compliant product images in large e-commerce catalogs.', 'Offensive content includes violent, sexually explicit, racist, or promotional images that harm customer experience and brand reputation.', 'The proposed system uses a combination of deep learning-based image classification and object detection techniques.', 'Challenges such as lack of training data, class imbalance, and fine-grained class definitions are tackled with unique technical strategies and budgeted crowdsourcing.', 'The solution is designed to handle extremely large, diverse, and evolving product image datasets in real-world retail environments.']
insight: Combining state-of-the-art image classification and object detection with budgeted crowdsourcing effectively addresses challenges like scarce training data, severe class imbalance, and fine-grained class definitions in large-scale offensive image detection for e-commerce.
impact: This system improves the quality and compliance of product images in e-commerce catalogs, enhancing customer experience, reducing legal risks, and protecting brand reputation across massive and diverse product inventories.

title: Semantic Referee: A Neural-Symbolic Framework for Enhancing Geospatial Semantic Segmentation
content: Understanding why machine learning algorithms may fail is usually the task of the human expert that uses domain knowledge and contextual information to discover systematic shortcomings in either the data or the algorithm. In this paper, we propose a semantic referee, which is able to extract qualitative features of the errors emerging from deep machine learning frameworks and suggest corrections. The semantic referee relies on ontological reasoning about spatial knowledge in order to characterize errors in terms of their spatial relations with the environment. Using semantics, the reasoner interacts with the learning algorithm as a supervisor. In this paper, the proposed method of the interaction between a neural network classifier and a semantic referee shows how to improve the performance of semantic segmentation for satellite imagery data.
summary: ['The paper introduces a semantic referee system that identifies and explains errors in deep learning models using ontological reasoning about spatial knowledge.', 'This semantic referee extracts qualitative features of errors and suggests corrections by analyzing spatial relations between errors and their environment.', 'The approach enables the semantic referee to act as a supervisor that interacts with neural network classifiers to improve their performance.', 'The method is demonstrated on semantic segmentation tasks involving satellite imagery data, showing improved results.', 'The work highlights the integration of symbolic reasoning with deep learning to enhance error understanding and correction.']
insight: By leveraging ontological reasoning about spatial relationships, the semantic referee can qualitatively characterize errors in deep learning outputs and provide corrective feedback, effectively supervising and improving neural network classifiers.
impact: This approach bridges symbolic reasoning and deep learning, enabling more interpretable and correctable AI systems, particularly enhancing semantic segmentation in satellite imagery, which is critical for environmental monitoring and geospatial analysis.

title: Bayesian policy selection using active inference
content: Learning to take actions based on observations is a core requirement for artificial agents to be able to be successful and robust at their task. Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a well-known technique for learning such policies. However, current RL algorithms often have to deal with reward shaping, have difficulties generalizing to other environments and are most often sample inefficient. In this paper, we explore active inference and the free energy principle, a normative theory from neuroscience that explains how self-organizing biological systems operate by maintaining a model of the world and casting action selection as an inference problem. We apply this concept to a typical problem known to the RL community, the mountain car problem, and show how active inference encompasses both RL and learning from demonstrations.
summary: ['The paper explores active inference and the free energy principle as an alternative to traditional reinforcement learning (RL) methods.', 'Active inference frames action selection as an inference problem, inspired by how biological systems maintain models of the world.', 'The approach is applied to the mountain car problem, a standard benchmark in RL research.', 'Active inference is shown to encompass both reinforcement learning and learning from demonstrations.', 'The method addresses common RL challenges such as reward shaping, generalization, and sample inefficiency.']
insight: Active inference treats action selection as a probabilistic inference problem based on maintaining a generative model of the environment, providing a unified framework that integrates reinforcement learning and learning from demonstrations while potentially improving generalization and sample efficiency.
impact: This approach offers a biologically inspired alternative to traditional RL that could overcome key limitations like reward shaping and poor generalization, potentially leading to more robust and efficient artificial agents.

title: I like fish, especially dolphins: Addressing Contradictions in Dialogue Modeling
content: To quantify how well natural language understanding models can capture consistency in a general conversation, we introduce the DialoguE COntradiction DEtection task (DECODE) and a new conversational dataset containing both human-human and human-bot contradictory dialogues. We then compare a structured utterance-based approach of using pre-trained Transformer models for contradiction detection with the typical unstructured approach. Results reveal that: (i) our newly collected dataset is notably more effective at providing supervision for the dialogue contradiction detection task than existing NLI data including those aimed to cover the dialogue domain; (ii) the structured utterance-based approach is more robust and transferable on both analysis and out-of-distribution dialogues than its unstructured counterpart. We also show that our best contradiction detection model correlates well with human judgments and further provide evidence for its usage in both automatically evaluating and improving the consistency of state-of-the-art generative chatbots.
summary: ['Introduced the DialoguE COntradiction DEtection (DECODE) task and a new conversational dataset with human-human and human-bot contradictory dialogues.', 'Compared structured utterance-based Transformer models to unstructured approaches for contradiction detection in dialogues.', 'Found the new dataset more effective for supervision than existing NLI datasets, including dialogue-focused ones.', 'Demonstrated that the structured approach is more robust and transferable across different dialogue types and out-of-distribution data.', 'Showed that the best contradiction detection model aligns well with human judgments and can be used to evaluate and improve chatbot consistency.']
insight: A structured utterance-based approach using pre-trained Transformer models, combined with a specialized contradiction dataset, significantly improves robustness and transferability in detecting contradictions in dialogues compared to unstructured methods.
impact: This work provides a more reliable method and dataset for evaluating and enhancing the consistency of conversational AI systems, which is critical for building trustworthy and coherent chatbots.

title: Compliance Generation for Privacy Documents under GDPR: A Roadmap for Implementing Automation and Machine Learning
content: Most prominent research today addresses compliance with data protection laws through consumer-centric and public-regulatory approaches. We shift this perspective with the Privatech project to focus on corporations and law firms as agents of compliance. To comply with data protection laws, data processors must implement accountability measures to assess and document compliance in relation to both privacy documents and privacy practices. In this paper, we survey, on the one hand, current research on GDPR automation, and on the other hand, the operational challenges corporations face to comply with GDPR, and that may benefit from new forms of automation. We attempt to bridge the gap. We provide a roadmap for compliance assessment and generation by identifying compliance issues, breaking them down into tasks that can be addressed through machine learning and automation, and providing notes about related developments in the Privatech project.
summary: ['The paper shifts focus from consumer-centric GDPR compliance to corporations and law firms as compliance agents.', 'It surveys current research on GDPR automation and operational challenges faced by corporations.', 'The authors propose a roadmap for compliance assessment and generation using machine learning and automation.', 'The paper identifies specific compliance issues and breaks them down into tasks suitable for automation.', 'It highlights developments within the Privatech project aimed at bridging gaps in GDPR compliance.']
insight: By decomposing GDPR compliance into discrete tasks, the paper demonstrates how machine learning and automation can operationalize accountability measures for data processors, enabling systematic assessment and documentation of compliance with privacy laws.
impact: This approach can significantly improve the efficiency and reliability of GDPR compliance for corporations and law firms, reducing legal risks and operational burdens while advancing automated regulatory adherence.

title: Neural document expansion for ad-hoc information retrieval
content: Recently, Nogueira et al. [2019] proposed a new approach to document expansion based on a neural Seq2Seq model, showing significant improvement on short text retrieval task. However, this approach needs a large amount of in-domain training data. In this paper, we show that this neural document expansion approach can be effectively adapted to standard IR tasks, where labels are scarce and many long documents are present.
summary: ['Nogueira et al. (2019) introduced a neural Seq2Seq model for document expansion that improves short text retrieval.', 'The original approach requires a large amount of in-domain training data.', 'The current paper demonstrates adapting this neural document expansion method to standard IR tasks with scarce labels and long documents.', 'This adaptation enables effective document expansion even in low-resource settings.']
insight: Neural Seq2Seq-based document expansion can be adapted to information retrieval tasks with limited labeled data and longer documents, overcoming the original method's dependency on large in-domain datasets.
impact: This adaptation broadens the applicability of neural document expansion techniques to more realistic IR scenarios, potentially improving retrieval performance where labeled data is limited and documents are lengthy.

title: Feature Selection and Feature Extraction in Pattern Analysis: A Literature Review
content: Pattern analysis often requires a pre-processing stage for extracting or selecting features in order to help the classification, prediction, or clustering stage discriminate or represent the data in a better way. The reason for this requirement is that the raw data are complex and difficult to process without extracting or selecting appropriate features beforehand. This paper reviews theory and motivation of different common methods of feature selection and extraction and introduces some of their applications. Some numerical implementations are also shown for these methods. Finally, the methods in feature selection and extraction are compared.
summary: ['The article reviews the theory and motivation behind various common feature selection and extraction methods.', 'It discusses the importance of pre-processing in pattern analysis to improve classification, prediction, and clustering.', 'Numerical implementations of these feature selection and extraction methods are presented.', 'A comparison of different feature selection and extraction techniques is provided.']
insight: Effective feature selection and extraction significantly enhance the ability of pattern analysis methods to discriminate and represent complex raw data, improving downstream tasks like classification and clustering.
impact: Understanding and applying appropriate feature selection and extraction methods can lead to more accurate and efficient data analysis, which is crucial in many AI and machine learning applications.

title: Conditioning LSTM Decoder and Bi-directional Attention Based Question Answering System
content: Applying neural-networks on Question Answering has gained increasing popularity in recent years. In this paper, I implemented a model with Bi-directional attention flow layer, connected with a Multi-layer LSTM encoder, connected with one start-index decoder and one conditioning end-index decoder. I introduce a new end-index decoder layer, conditioning on start-index output. The Experiment shows this has increased model performance by 15.16%. For prediction, I proposed a new smart-span equation, rewarding both short answer length and high probability in start-index and end-index, which further improved the prediction accuracy. The best single model achieves an F1 score of 73.97% and EM score of 64.95% on test set.
summary: ['Implemented a question answering model using a Bi-directional attention flow layer combined with a multi-layer LSTM encoder.', 'Introduced a novel end-index decoder layer conditioned on the start-index output, improving model performance by 15.16%.', 'Proposed a new smart-span prediction equation that balances short answer length and high probability for start and end indices, enhancing prediction accuracy.', 'Achieved a best single model F1 score of 73.97% and Exact Match (EM) score of 64.95% on the test set.']
insight: Conditioning the end-index decoder on the start-index output and using a smart-span prediction equation that rewards both short answer length and high probability significantly improves question answering model accuracy.
impact: This approach offers a more accurate and efficient method for span-based question answering, potentially advancing the performance of neural QA systems and influencing future model architectures.

title: Robust Federated Training via Collaborative Machine Teaching using Trusted Instances
content: Federated learning performs distributed model training using local data hosted by agents. It shares only model parameter updates for iterative aggregation at the server. Although it is privacy-preserving by design, federated learning is vulnerable to noise corruption of local agents, as demonstrated in the previous study on adversarial data poisoning threat against federated learning systems. Even a single noise-corrupted agent can bias the model training. In our work, we propose a collaborative and privacy-preserving machine teaching paradigm with multiple distributed teachers, to improve robustness of the federated training process against local data corruption. We assume that each local agent (teacher) have the resources to verify a small portions of trusted instances, which may not by itself be adequate for learning. In the proposed collaborative machine teaching method, these trusted instances guide the distributed agents to jointly select a compact while informative training subset from data hosted by their own. Simultaneously, the agents learn to add changes of limited magnitudes into the selected data instances, in order to improve the testing performances of the federally trained model despite of the training data corruption. Experiments on toy and real data demonstrate that our approach can identify training set bugs effectively and suggest appropriate changes to the labels. Our algorithm is a step toward trustworthy machine learning.
summary: ['Federated learning enables distributed model training by sharing only model updates, preserving privacy but vulnerable to noise corruption from local agents.', 'The paper proposes a collaborative, privacy-preserving machine teaching paradigm involving multiple distributed teachers to enhance robustness against local data corruption.', 'Each local agent verifies a small portion of trusted instances to guide the selection of a compact, informative training subset from their own data.', 'Agents learn to make limited-magnitude changes to selected data instances to improve model testing performance despite corrupted training data.', 'Experiments on synthetic and real datasets show the approach effectively identifies training set bugs and suggests label corrections.']
insight: By leveraging multiple distributed teachers who verify trusted data subsets and collaboratively select and adjust training data, the method improves federated learning robustness against adversarial noise and data poisoning without compromising privacy.
impact: This approach enhances the trustworthiness and reliability of federated learning systems, which are critical for privacy-sensitive applications, by mitigating the risk of corrupted local data biasing the global model.

title: Image Matters: Scalable Detection of Offensive and Non-Compliant Content / Logo in Product Images
content: In e-commerce, product content, especially product images have a significant influence on a customer's journey from product discovery to evaluation and finally, purchase decision. Since many e-commerce retailers sell items from other third-party marketplace sellers besides their own, the content published by both internal and external content creators needs to be monitored and enriched, wherever possible. Despite guidelines and warnings, product listings that contain offensive and non-compliant images continue to enter catalogs. Offensive and non-compliant content can include a wide range of objects, logos, and banners conveying violent, sexually explicit, racist, or promotional messages. Such images can severely damage the customer experience, lead to legal issues, and erode the company brand. In this paper, we present a computer vision driven offensive and non-compliant image detection system for extremely large image datasets. This paper delves into the unique challenges of applying deep learning to real-world product image data from retail world. We demonstrate how we resolve a number of technical challenges such as lack of training data, severe class imbalance, fine-grained class definitions etc. using a number of practical yet unique technical strategies. Our system combines state-of-the-art image classification and object detection techniques with budgeted crowdsourcing to develop a solution customized for a massive, diverse, and constantly evolving product catalog.
summary: ['E-commerce product images significantly impact customer purchase decisions, necessitating monitoring and enrichment of content from both internal and third-party sellers.', 'Offensive and non-compliant images, including violent, explicit, racist, or promotional content, continue to appear despite guidelines, harming customer experience and brand reputation.', 'The paper presents a computer vision system designed to detect offensive and non-compliant images in extremely large e-commerce image datasets.', 'Key technical challenges addressed include lack of training data, severe class imbalance, and fine-grained class definitions.', 'The solution integrates state-of-the-art image classification and object detection with budgeted crowdsourcing to handle a massive, diverse, and evolving product catalog.']
insight: Combining advanced image classification and object detection techniques with budgeted crowdsourcing effectively addresses challenges like data scarcity, class imbalance, and fine-grained categorization in large-scale offensive image detection for e-commerce.
impact: This system helps e-commerce platforms maintain compliant and safe product catalogs, protecting customer experience, reducing legal risks, and preserving brand integrity amid vast and diverse third-party content.

title: Semantic Referee: A Neural-Symbolic Framework for Enhancing Geospatial Semantic Segmentation
content: Understanding why machine learning algorithms may fail is usually the task of the human expert that uses domain knowledge and contextual information to discover systematic shortcomings in either the data or the algorithm. In this paper, we propose a semantic referee, which is able to extract qualitative features of the errors emerging from deep machine learning frameworks and suggest corrections. The semantic referee relies on ontological reasoning about spatial knowledge in order to characterize errors in terms of their spatial relations with the environment. Using semantics, the reasoner interacts with the learning algorithm as a supervisor. In this paper, the proposed method of the interaction between a neural network classifier and a semantic referee shows how to improve the performance of semantic segmentation for satellite imagery data.
summary: ['The paper introduces a semantic referee system that identifies and explains errors in deep learning models using ontological reasoning about spatial knowledge.', 'The semantic referee extracts qualitative features of errors and suggests corrections by analyzing spatial relations in the environment.', 'This approach enables the semantic referee to act as a supervisor interacting with the neural network classifier.', 'The method is demonstrated to improve semantic segmentation performance on satellite imagery data.']
insight: Integrating ontological reasoning about spatial relationships as a semantic referee allows for qualitative error characterization and correction, enhancing deep learning model performance through supervised interaction.
impact: This approach provides a novel way to diagnose and correct systematic errors in machine learning models, particularly improving accuracy in complex tasks like satellite image segmentation, which can benefit applications in remote sensing and geospatial analysis.

title: Bayesian policy selection using active inference
content: Learning to take actions based on observations is a core requirement for artificial agents to be able to be successful and robust at their task. Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a well-known technique for learning such policies. However, current RL algorithms often have to deal with reward shaping, have difficulties generalizing to other environments and are most often sample inefficient. In this paper, we explore active inference and the free energy principle, a normative theory from neuroscience that explains how self-organizing biological systems operate by maintaining a model of the world and casting action selection as an inference problem. We apply this concept to a typical problem known to the RL community, the mountain car problem, and show how active inference encompasses both RL and learning from demonstrations.
summary: ['The paper explores active inference and the free energy principle as an alternative to traditional reinforcement learning (RL) methods.', 'Active inference treats action selection as an inference problem, inspired by how biological systems self-organize and maintain world models.', 'The approach is applied to the mountain car problem, a standard benchmark in RL research.', 'Active inference is shown to encompass both reinforcement learning and learning from demonstrations, potentially addressing issues like reward shaping and sample inefficiency.']
insight: Active inference reframes action selection as a probabilistic inference problem based on maintaining a generative model of the environment, which can unify reinforcement learning and learning from demonstrations under a single theoretical framework.
impact: This approach could lead to more robust and sample-efficient learning algorithms that generalize better across environments, overcoming key limitations of current RL methods.
